text.title: Prompt PR Review Checklist — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `prompt-pr-review-checklist` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a checklist from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: Items are either READ-DO (perform action, then tick) or DO-CONFIRM (perform task, then verify ticked). The mode is named in the checklist header.

rationale.source: Atul Gawande, The Checklist Manifesto; Boeing flight-deck procedures

rule.rationale: Mixing modes is the single most common cause of checklist failure in operating rooms and on flight decks. Naming the mode forces the right cognitive routine.

rule.statement: Each pause-point holds 5–9 items. Longer pause-points are split into named sub-points.
rule.rationale: Working-memory caps mean a 20-item checklist routinely loses items 11–18. Pause-point sizing is the difference between a tool that works and a tool that fails silently.

rule.statement: Every item must defend a real failure that has occurred or has a credible model. Decorative items ('confirm intent') are removed during quarterly review.

rationale.source: Reason's Swiss-cheese model of accident causation; Gawande's surgical-checklist study

rule.rationale: Checklists rot when items accumulate without removal. Each item earns its slot by pointing to an incident, a postmortem, or a regulator's finding.

rule.statement: Each item carries an executor role and the artefact that completes it (link, screenshot, signed sign-off). Items without artefacts are ambiguous and drop.
rule.rationale: Anonymous items are 'somebody else's job' — exactly the failure pattern checklists are designed to prevent.

rule.statement: The checklist has a named owner, a `last_reviewed` field, and a quarterly review ritual. Lapsed checklists are auto-flagged as stale.

rationale.source: ISO 9001 document-control practice; Diátaxis maintainability model

rule.rationale: Operating environments change faster than templates. Without a review cadence, checklists drift into obsolescence and lose authority.
